_disclaimer: ALL ITEMS IN THIS FILE ARE SYNTHETIC PLACEHOLDERS, hand-authored to exercise the LSAT Item contract. They are NOT real LSAT items and must NOT be used as study material. Real items must be sourced from official LSAC PrepTests with redistribution licensing (D-SR11). Every item carries SyntheticFlag=SYNTHETIC.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] A coffee shop raised its prices last month, yet its total revenue from coffee increased even though it sold fewer cups than before.
items.Stem: Which one of the following, if true, most helps to resolve the apparent discrepancy?
items.ChoiceA: The shop also began selling pastries for the first time last month.
items.ChoiceB: Several customers complained about the price increase.
items.ChoiceC: The shop reduced its opening hours last month.
items.ChoiceD: A competing café nearby closed down last month.
items.ChoiceE: The higher price per cup more than offset the smaller number of cups sold.
items.WhyWrongA: Pastry sales explain total store revenue, not coffee revenue specifically.
items.WhyWrongB: Complaints do not explain how revenue rose.
items.WhyWrongC: Fewer hours would tend to lower sales, deepening the puzzle.
items.WhyWrongD: A closed competitor would raise cups sold, contradicting 'fewer cups.'
items.WhyWrongE: CORRECT. A large enough price rise can raise coffee revenue despite fewer cups sold.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] Despite eating a diet high in cheese and butter, the people of a certain region have unusually low rates of heart disease.
items.Stem: Which one of the following, if true, does most to resolve the apparent paradox?
items.ChoiceA: Residents of the region walk many miles each day and eat large amounts of fresh vegetables.
items.ChoiceB: Heart disease is the leading cause of death worldwide.
items.ChoiceC: The region imports most of its dairy products from abroad.
items.ChoiceD: Cheese and butter are among the region's most popular foods.
items.ChoiceE: The region's cuisine is admired by chefs internationally.
items.WhyWrongA: CORRECT. Heavy activity and vegetables supply a protective factor offsetting the rich diet.
items.WhyWrongB: Worldwide statistics do not explain this region's low rate.
items.WhyWrongC: Where dairy comes from does not affect its health impact.
items.WhyWrongD: This restates the puzzling fact without resolving it.
items.WhyWrongE: Culinary reputation is irrelevant to heart-disease rates.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] The library installed self-checkout machines to reduce staff workload, but staff now report being busier than before.
items.Stem: Which one of the following, if true, most helps to explain the surprising result?
items.ChoiceA: Since the machines were installed, library visits have risen sharply, increasing all staff duties.
items.ChoiceB: The self-checkout machines were cheaper than hiring additional staff.
items.ChoiceC: Some patrons still prefer to check out books with a staff member.
items.ChoiceD: The machines occasionally jam and need a quick reset.
items.ChoiceE: Staff attended a one-time training session on the machines.
items.WhyWrongA: CORRECT. A surge in visitors raises other duties, so staff are busier despite the machines.
items.WhyWrongB: Cost savings do not explain increased busyness.
items.WhyWrongC: A minority preferring staff checkout is too small to explain the effect.
items.WhyWrongD: Occasional resets are minor and would not outweigh the labor saved.
items.WhyWrongE: One-time training does not explain ongoing greater busyness.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] A neighborhood restaurant raised the prices on its menu last month, yet the number of customers it served each day increased.
items.Stem: Which one of the following, if true, most helps to resolve the apparent discrepancy?
items.ChoiceA: Several longtime customers complained about the higher prices.
items.ChoiceB: The restaurant reduced its portion sizes at the same time it raised prices.
items.ChoiceC: The restaurant's owner had been considering a price increase for several months.
items.ChoiceD: Around the time of the price increase, the restaurant was featured in a popular food magazine, attracting many new diners.
items.ChoiceE: The higher prices reduced the restaurant's profit margin on each dish.
items.WhyWrongA: Complaints, if anything, make the rise in customers more puzzling.
items.WhyWrongB: Smaller portions with higher prices would tend to drive customers away, deepening the puzzle.
items.WhyWrongC: How long the increase was contemplated does not explain why customers rose.
items.WhyWrongD: CORRECT. A well-timed magazine feature brought in enough new diners to more than offset any loss from higher prices, explaining the rise in customers.
items.WhyWrongE: A lower margin per dish does not bear on the number of customers.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] The new highway was built to shorten travel time between the two cities, but average travel time between them has actually increased since it opened.
items.Stem: Which one of the following, if true, does most to resolve the apparent paradox?
items.ChoiceA: The old road between the two cities remains open to traffic.
items.ChoiceB: The new highway has a higher speed limit than the old road did.
items.ChoiceC: The highway made the route so convenient that far more drivers now use it, producing heavy congestion.
items.ChoiceD: The highway cost considerably more to build than had been projected.
items.ChoiceE: Travel time by train between the two cities has decreased.
items.WhyWrongA: That the old road is open does not explain longer times on the new highway.
items.WhyWrongB: A higher speed limit should shorten travel time, deepening the puzzle.
items.WhyWrongC: CORRECT. Induced demand — many more drivers using the convenient new route — causes congestion that raises average travel time despite the highway's design.
items.WhyWrongD: Construction cost has nothing to do with travel time.
items.WhyWrongE: Train times concern a different mode and do not resolve the driving paradox.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] The company doubled its advertising budget last year, yet its total sales for the year came out exactly the same as the year before.
items.Stem: Which one of the following, if true, most helps to resolve the apparent discrepancy?
items.ChoiceA: The company sold the same products last year as in the prior year.
items.ChoiceB: The company's new advertisements won a national industry award.
items.ChoiceC: The company's advertising budget is now larger than that of any competitor.
items.ChoiceD: Most of the additional advertising ran on social-media platforms.
items.ChoiceE: A strong new competitor entered the market last year; without the extra advertising, the company's sales would have fallen sharply.
items.WhyWrongA: Selling the same products does not account for the puzzle.
items.WhyWrongB: An award does not explain why sales stayed flat.
items.WhyWrongC: Having the largest budget makes flat sales, if anything, more puzzling.
items.WhyWrongD: Where the ads ran does not explain the lack of sales growth.
items.WhyWrongE: CORRECT. The advertising held sales steady in the face of a new competitor that would otherwise have taken market share, explaining the flat figure.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] People who take vitamin C supplements report just as many colds as people who take none, even though vitamin C is known to support immune function.
items.Stem: Which one of the following, if true, most helps to resolve the apparent discrepancy?
items.ChoiceA: Vitamin C supplements are inexpensive and widely available.
items.ChoiceB: The common cold can be caused by a wide variety of different viruses.
items.ChoiceC: People are most likely to begin taking vitamin C precisely when they feel a cold coming on.
items.ChoiceD: Studies show that vitamin C shortens the duration of a cold once it has begun.
items.ChoiceE: People who take vitamin C supplements tend to be older than those who do not.
items.WhyWrongA: Price and availability do not explain the equal cold counts.
items.WhyWrongB: The variety of cold viruses does not explain why supplements make no difference.
items.WhyWrongC: CORRECT. If people start vitamin C only when a cold is imminent, supplement users are a group already prone to colds, masking any preventive benefit.
items.WhyWrongD: Shortening a cold's duration makes vitamin C's apparent lack of effect on frequency more puzzling.
items.WhyWrongE: Age differences are not tied to the reported cold counts in a way that resolves the puzzle.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] The factory replaced its machines with new ones that each work twice as fast, yet the factory's total daily output has not risen at all.
items.Stem: Which one of the following, if true, most helps to explain the surprising result?
items.ChoiceA: Every product must pass through a single inspection station whose speed did not change and which sets the pace for the entire line.
items.ChoiceB: The new machines are considerably more energy-efficient than the old ones.
items.ChoiceC: Installing the new machines cost more than the factory had budgeted.
items.ChoiceD: In testing, the new machines produced twice as many parts per hour as the old ones.
items.ChoiceE: The factory produces the same goods it did before the machines were replaced.
items.WhyWrongA: CORRECT. A single unchanged bottleneck station caps total output regardless of how fast the other machines run, explaining the flat production.
items.WhyWrongB: Energy efficiency does not bear on output volume.
items.WhyWrongC: Cost overruns are irrelevant to output.
items.WhyWrongD: Faster machines in testing make the flat output more puzzling, not less.
items.WhyWrongE: Producing the same goods does not explain the lack of increase.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] Despite receiving far less annual rainfall than the neighboring valley, the plateau supports much denser vegetation than the valley does.
items.Stem: Which one of the following, if true, does most to resolve the apparent paradox?
items.ChoiceA: The valley receives more rainfall in a typical year than the plateau does.
items.ChoiceB: The plateau's soil retains water far more effectively, and its cooler climate sharply reduces evaporation.
items.ChoiceC: Rainfall across the whole region varies noticeably from one year to the next.
items.ChoiceD: The neighboring valley is a popular destination for weekend hikers.
items.ChoiceE: The valley is home to several rare species of wildflower.
items.WhyWrongA: This merely restates the puzzling premise rather than resolving it.
items.WhyWrongB: CORRECT. Better water retention and less evaporation let the plateau make far more of its limited rain, supporting dense growth despite low rainfall.
items.WhyWrongC: Year-to-year variation affects both areas and does not resolve the contrast.
items.WhyWrongD: Hikers do not explain the plateau's denser vegetation.
items.WhyWrongE: Rare species in the valley do not explain the plateau's greater density.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] The clinic sees more patients per day than it did five years ago, yet its total annual revenue has fallen over the same period.
items.Stem: Which one of the following, if true, most helps to resolve the apparent discrepancy?
items.ChoiceA: The clinic has hired several additional nurses over the past five years.
items.ChoiceB: Patient satisfaction with the clinic has improved over the period.
items.ChoiceC: The clinic now treats more patients per day than any other clinic in the area.
items.ChoiceD: Five years ago most visits were costly procedures, whereas today the great majority are brief, low-fee checkups.
items.ChoiceE: The clinic is open for the same number of hours each week as it was five years ago.
items.WhyWrongA: Hiring nurses concerns costs, not the revenue-versus-patients puzzle.
items.WhyWrongB: Higher satisfaction does not explain the drop in revenue.
items.WhyWrongC: Seeing more patients than rivals deepens, not resolves, the revenue puzzle.
items.WhyWrongD: CORRECT. A shift from expensive procedures to inexpensive checkups can lower revenue even as the patient count rises.
items.WhyWrongE: Unchanged hours do not explain falling revenue with more patients.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] A new law made it substantially easier for tenants to sue their landlords, but the number of such lawsuits has fallen since the law took effect.
items.Stem: Which one of the following, if true, most helps to explain the surprising result?
items.ChoiceA: The number of tenants in the region has stayed roughly constant.
items.ChoiceB: Many tenants had been unaware of their rights under the earlier rules.
items.ChoiceC: The new law also increased the damages a tenant could be awarded in court.
items.ChoiceD: Because the law strengthened tenants' legal position, most landlords now resolve disputes promptly rather than risk a losing lawsuit.
items.ChoiceE: The new law was debated in the legislature for several years before it passed.
items.WhyWrongA: A stable tenant population does not account for the decline.
items.WhyWrongB: Prior ignorance of rights would suggest more suits after the law, not fewer.
items.WhyWrongC: Larger potential awards would encourage more lawsuits, deepening the puzzle.
items.WhyWrongD: CORRECT. If landlords now settle quickly to avoid near-certain losses, fewer disputes reach court, so lawsuits fall even though suing is easier.
items.WhyWrongE: The length of the debate does not explain the drop in lawsuits.
